questions.question: What happens when you assign a String from one variable to another?
options: Both variables point to the same data
questions.explanation: In Rust, assigning a String moves ownership. The original variable is invalidated.

questions.question: You can have multiple mutable references to the same data at the same time.
questions.explanation: Rust's borrowing rules allow either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references, never both.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to make this code compile:
questions.explanation: clone() creates a deep copy, so both s1 and s2 are valid.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a valid way to pass a String to a function without transferring ownership?
questions.explanation: Passing String directly transfers ownership. References (&String, &str) borrow without taking ownership.

questions.question: Types that implement the Copy trait are moved instead of copied when assigned.
questions.explanation: Copy types (like i32, bool, char) are automatically copied on assignment, not moved.

questions.question: What does the borrow checker prevent?
questions.explanation: The borrow checker's primary job is preventing data races by enforcing reference rules at compile time.

questions.question: Fix this code by adding the correct lifetime annotation:
questions.explanation: The return type needs a lifetime annotation to tell Rust the reference's validity scope.

questions.question: A slice (&[T]) takes ownership of the data it references.
questions.explanation: Slices are references -- they borrow a contiguous section of a collection without owning it.
